Center: OECD/NEA Nuclear Data Bank, Boulogne-Billancourt, France
ref: Jour: Jour. of Nuclear Science and Technology, Vol.61, p.1385 (2024)
title: Measurement of the thermal neutron capture-cross section of 191Ir at ANNRI MLF J-PARC

x4freetext: Measurement of the thermal neutron capture-cross
section of 191Ir at ANNRI MLF J-PARC

x4codes.shortRef: Jour: Jour. of Nuclear Science and Technology, Vol.61, p.1385 (2024)

x4freetext: ANNRI(BL04) beam line of MLF at J-PARC
Spallation reaction in a mercury target
irradiated with 3-GeV protons. Produced neutrons were
slowed down in a supercritical para hydrogen
moderator (140 mm in diam., 120 mm in height) .
The proton beams were supplied at a repetition rate of
25 Hz and under a proton beam power of 835 kW.
Disk chopper with a semicircular shape was used to cut
the low-energy tail of neutron pulse.
The relative neutron spectrum was reconstructed
using the 478-keV gamma-rays from 10B(n,alpha1) 7Li
reaction.
An enriched 191Ir metal powder with an isotopic
purity of 99.904% was used. The Ir powder was pressed
into a pellet shape with a diameter of 5 mm in
diameter and a thickness of 1 mm.
A B4C sample containing enriched 10B was used for
neutron spectrum measurement.
A natural carbon sample was used as neutron scatterer
to subtract background.
A gold sample with a diameter of 5 mm and a thickness
of 0.1 mm was used for normalization.
Flight path length of 27.9 m.
The NaI(Tl) spectrometer was used consisting
of two different-sized NaI(Tl) detectors. The larger
detector: 330 mm (diam.) and 203 mm long, placed at
90 deg. with respect to the neutron beam. The smaller
detector: 203mm (diam.) and 203 mm long, placed at
125 deg. with respect to the neutron beam.
Pulse-height weighting technique (PHWT) was applied.
Measurements with the carbon sample and
"Blank sample (no sample) " were used for background
estimation. The relative neutron spectrum was
estimated using the boron sample. The weighting
function was normalized by comparing the experimental
results of the Au sample with the capture cross
section in JENDL-5 from 17 to 40 meV.
Dead Time Correction was applied with the extended
dead-time model. Correction factors for self
shielding and multiple scattering were calculated
using the Monte-Carlo simulation code PHITS.
Uncertainty due to normalization
Uncertainty due to PHWT
Uncertainty due to sample mass
